articles.title: The JavaScript Bundler Grand Prix
articles.description: Bundlers now sit at the heart of many JavaScript workflows and are sometimes even integrated into runtimes (e.g. Bun’s). This piece surveys the landscape and argues the speed wars are mostly over, with the real battle shifting to artifact size and the code that actually ships to users.
articles.author: Kate Holterhoff

articles.title: 'I Ported JustHTML from Python to JavaScript with LLMs in 4.5 Hours'
articles.description: Prolific AI blogger Simon Willison shares the tale of porting a standards-compliant HTML5 parser (which passes all 9200+ html5lib-tests) from Python to JavaScript using OpenAI’s Codex CLI and GPT 5.2. You can play with the end result or check out the code.
articles.author: Simon Willison

articles.title: 1. A Perplexing JavaScript Parsing Puzzle
articles.description: Hillel's deceptively simple puzzle – just 14 bytes of code – attracted by far the most attention this year. Despite working with JavaScript for most of its lifespan, I got it wrong!
articles.author: Hillel Wayne

articles.title: 2. Ecma International Approved ECMAScript 2025: What’s New?
articles.description: Each year, the Ecma General Assembly approves the latest ECMAScript language specification, and you can read the ES2025 spec in full. Better, though, is to enjoy Dr. Axel’s succinct explainer.
articles.author: Dr. Axel Rauschmayer

articles.title: 3. 'I Think the Ergonomics of Generators is Growing on Me'
articles.description: If you've never worked with generator functions, this remains a great look at what they are and where they can be useful. Alex notes that “their practicality hasn’t exactly caught on” (yet..)
articles.author: Alex MacArthur

articles.title: 4. How the Web is Using JavaScript
articles.description: The JavaScript section of this year's HTTP Archive Web Almanac report went into depth on how much JS we’re using (and failing to use), the popularity of TypeScript, Web Worker usage, and.. yes, jQuery still dominates!
articles.author: HTTP Archive

articles.title: 5. Some Features Every JavaScript Developer Should Know in 2025
articles.description: A quick list post breezing through some more modern areas of JavaScript including iterator helpers, structuredClone(), and set operations.
articles.author: Suren Enfiajyan

articles.description: 🛠️ Dan Abramov shows off his new 'RSC Explorer', a tool for exploring how React Server Components work behind the scenes.
Cloudflare Workers' Wrangler tool now supports automatic configuration for deploying apps using numerous web frameworks to Workers, including Next.js, Astro, TanStack Start, SvelteKit, and Nuxt.
picknplace.js offers a fresh take on the idea of drag-and-drop.
🤖 OpenAI has revealed its GPT 5.2 Codex model and we learn how two recent React vulnerabilities were discovered with its help.
Tesseract.js 7.0 – OCR library to extract text from images; now faster.
Base UI 1.0 – Elegant React component suite from a strong team.
Wasp 0.20 – Rails-like framework for React 19, Node & Prisma.
Graffle 7.4 – JavaScript GraphQL client that runs anywhere.
Next.js 16.1, Bun 1.3.5, MathJax 4.1, Prisma 7.2

articles.title: 01: Six CSS Snippets Every Frontend Developer Should Know
articles.description: Adam shared a similarly popular list last year, so it's of little surprise to see the idea prove popular again. A grab bag of sound snippets worth knowing which are “bigger, more powerful, and leverage progressive enhancement a bit more”.
articles.author: Adam Argyle

articles.title: 02: Default Styles for H1 Elements are Changing
articles.description: We shared this back in April — it outlined how browsers were updating the default user agent (UA) styles for nested section headings. A high-level change that many of you wanted to be on top of.
articles.author: Simon Pieters (MDN)

articles.title: 03: You're Not a Front-end Developer Until You've...
articles.description: This fun, tongue-in-cheek checklist of the various oddities we all do as frontend devs is still amusing now.
articles.author: Nic Chan

articles.title: 04: What You Need to Know About Modern CSS
articles.description: Chris runs through a list of things which are now valuable to know either because they are “pretty fresh or have enjoyed a boost in browser support”. Covers things such as the popover attribute, the powerful if() function, the new field-sizing property, and more. An excellent overview of what new stuff we should be paying attention to, along with some pointers as to what’s on the horizon.
articles.author: Chris Coyier

articles.title: 05: The <select> Element Can Now Be Customized with CSS
articles.description: Back in March we got an accessible, standardized way to create styleable <select> elements — thanks to appearance: base-select. Adam explained how this change had been “many years in the making”. The result? We now have a rich component that won’t break in older browsers.
articles.author: Adam Argyle

articles.title: 06: CSS text-box-trim
articles.description: Another from Adam, this time explaning how this handy property works, letting us have fine control over the leading space found both above and below text.
articles.author: Adam Argyle

articles.title: 07: Breadcrumbs are 'Dead' in Web Design
articles.description: Once a clear staple in web design, the author argued that they’ve now become somewhat obsolete.
articles.author: Noah Davis

articles.title: 08: Cool Native HTML Elements You Should Already Be Using
articles.description: A roundup of numerous, native HTML elements that don't quite see as much usage relative to others (such as kbd, datalist, progress, and more).
articles.author: Harrison Broadbent

articles.title: 09: A Guide to Scroll-Driven Animations with Just CSS
articles.description: Back in the summer, Saron shared this look at how to start using scroll-driven animations via CSS. Code examples shared here highlight how this technique can be used to create progress bars or animate elements into the viewport.
articles.author: Saron Yitbarek

articles.title: 10: "I’m More Proud of These 128 Kilobytes Than Anything I’ve Built Since"
articles.description: A good look back at a project and how strict constraints (such as bandwidth, processing power, etc) can often result in innovation. A popular, and technically rich, reminder to us all to consider the widest range of users’ and to design things accordingly.
articles.author: Mike Hall
